algorithms.title: Pediatric Shock Recognition
algorithms.description: Sequence for recognizing shock from perfusion findings rather than waiting for hypotension.
steps: Complete the PAT, noting Appearance and Circulation to Skin.
Assess perfusion directly: capillary refill, skin color/temperature, peripheral pulse quality compared to central pulses, mental status.
Check heart rate for tachycardia out of proportion to fever/anxiety.
Check blood pressure for age — remember normal BP does not exclude shock.
If perfusion findings are abnormal with normal BP: compensated shock — begin treatment now, do not wait for hypotension.
If perfusion findings are abnormal with low BP for age: decompensated shock — treat as a time-critical emergency and escalate immediately.
Reassess perfusion frequently throughout care and transport.

algorithms.title: Category-Matched Shock Treatment
algorithms.description: Matching the suspected shock category to the appropriate field intervention.
steps: Gather history and exam clues to identify the likely shock category: fluid/blood loss (hypovolemic), fever or allergen exposure (distributive), cardiac history (cardiogenic), trauma with unilateral chest findings (obstructive).
Hypovolemic or septic (distributive): administer isotonic fluid bolus per local protocol; reassess perfusion after each bolus.
Anaphylactic (distributive): administer epinephrine as the priority treatment.
Cardiogenic: give fluids cautiously, watching for signs of volume overload; avoid aggressive bolusing.
Obstructive from tension pneumothorax: perform needle decompression per protocol/scope of practice.
In all categories: supplemental oxygen, positioning, keep the child warm, and rapid transport.
For advanced resuscitation and drug dosing depth, refer to the PALS reviewer.
